nativeRange.habitat: Tropical reef-associated waters, typically 0–170 m depth; commonly seen on coral reefs, lagoons, and seaward slopes

diet.notes: In the wild feeds mainly on nudibranchs, tubeworm tentacles, other benthic invertebrates, algae, and coral polyps. In captivity is generally weaned onto a varied diet of mysis shrimp, finely chopped seafood, frozen marine preparations, and vitamin-enriched flake; offering a small anemone has been reported to help stimulate initial feeding in reluctant individuals. Multiple small feedings per day are typically advised.

avoidWith: other Chaetodon butterflyfishes (in tanks under ~750 L)
small ornamental shrimp and other delicate invertebrates

summary: The Raccoon Butterflyfish (Chaetodon lunula) is a widespread Indo-Pacific species recognized by its bold black eye-mask. Generally peaceful toward other fish, it tends to be territorial toward its own kind and is not reef-safe, making it best suited to fish-only marine systems.
careNotes: The Raccoon Butterflyfish is one of the more adaptable members of its family for fish-only marine aquariums, but it is not a beginner species — stable water chemistry, consistent water quality, and a varied diet are generally advised. It is commonly considered peaceful toward other species but has been reported to be territorial toward its own kind and toward similar-looking butterflyfishes; it is best kept singly or as a verified mated pair, with multiple specimens only attempted in much larger systems. It is not reef-safe: the species has been documented eating coral polyps, anemones, ornamental shrimp, feather dusters, and other stationary invertebrates. Juveniles tend to be more active at night, with activity gradually shifting toward daytime as they mature. Like other butterflyfishes, this species is commonly considered susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) — a condition that causes pitting and discoloration along the head and the sensory line running down the body — when kept in poor water quality or fed an insufficiently varied, vitamin-rich, algae-inclusive diet. Wild populations are reported as monogamous, so established pairs should not be separated. Initial weaning onto frozen foods can occasionally be difficult; offering live mysis shrimp or a small anemone has been reported to help stimulate feeding.
breedingNotes: The Raccoon Butterflyfish is egg-scattering and forms monogamous pairs, with reports suggesting the bond may be both a matter of social preference and a biological requirement. Captive breeding of Chaetodon butterflyfishes is generally considered impractical for hobbyists; the larvae spend their early life drifting in open water and are difficult to rear, and there are no widely-reported captive-bred lines for this species.
